meta.title: AI-based model discovery for active distribution networks

slides.headline: AI-based model discovery for active distribution networks
slides.subheadline: Physics-informed candidate ranking for low-voltage topology reconstruction from smart-meter data.
slides.note: Open with the central aim: reconstructing LV radial topology using voltage and current data.

slides.headline: Four steps from grid problem to model direction
slides.subheadline: The story moves from operational need, to formulation, to model design, to results and next steps.

cards.title: Introduction
cards.body: Dynamic LV networks, operational needs, and project aims.

cards.title: Problem Formulation
cards.body: Literature gap, smart-meter data, and preprocessing pipeline.

cards.title: Model Architecture
cards.body: Candidate ranking compared with direct edge prediction.

cards.title: Results & Next Steps
cards.body: Test performance, physics direction, and robustness plans.

slides.note: Use this as the signposting slide.

slides.headline: Turn smart-meter signals into actionable grid topology
slides.subheadline: The project reconstructs low-voltage radial network structure from voltage and current measurements.
bullets: Reconstruct LV radial topology from meter data.
Bridge raw measurements and infrastructure maps.
slides.note: Stress that the available evidence is electrical measurement data, not perfect network records.

slides.headline: LV networks are changing faster than records can keep up
slides.subheadline: Accurate topology is fundamental for monitoring, planning, state estimation, and control.
slides.note: Frame this as an operational need, not just an ML exercise.

slides.headline: The gap is learned, physics-informed candidate ranking
slides.subheadline: Prior work covers smart-meter topology identification and candidate-based reconstruction, but not this exact combination.
bullets: Question: can electrical and physics signals rank candidates?
slides.note: Mention Liu et al. as motivation for candidate topology reconstruction, then define the gap clearly.

slides.headline: Each phase becomes one independent topology example
slides.subheadline: The model maps aligned voltage/current measurements and candidate graphs to a selected radial topology.

cards.title: Model inputs
cards.body: Voltage_Gk.csv, Current_RE_Gk.csv, Current_IM_Gk.csv, Vtr_save.mat, and nmi_k.csv.

cards.title: Ground truth
cards.body: A_base_k.csv provides the target adjacency matrix for evaluation.

cards.title: Target output
cards.body: A selected radial topology represented as a binary adjacency matrix.

slides.note: Keep this concrete: what files go in, what structure comes out.

slides.headline: A strict pipeline turns feeder files into trainable examples
slides.subheadline: Filtering protects the candidate ranker from malformed data and weakly observed cases.

steps.title: Align
steps.body: Use NMI mapping to match nodes.

slides.note: Mention mapping, integrity, and observability as the three main filters.

slides.headline: Directly predicting edges struggled to produce valid networks
slides.subheadline: A local node-pair prediction task does not naturally enforce global radial structure.

left.title: Initial approach
bullets: Predict edge probability for node pairs.

slides.note: Use this to justify why the project moved to candidate ranking.

slides.headline: Rank whole radial structures instead of isolated edges
slides.subheadline: Each candidate topology receives a scalar plausibility score, then the highest-ranked graph is selected.

steps.title: Score
steps.body: Use a neural model for plausibility.

slides.note: The paradigm shift is candidate topology to plausibility score.

slides.headline: Separate encoders combine measurement, source, and candidate evidence
slides.subheadline: The combined MLP head outputs one scalar candidate score for ranking non-differentiable graph structures.

cards.title: Node & source
cards.body: Voltage/current statistics, real and imaginary current metrics, observed-node flags, and source trends.

cards.title: Candidate setup
cards.body: Edge counts, degree statistics, graph depth, observed-edge ratios, MST scores, and physics consistency.

cards.title: Neural model
cards.body: Node encoder MLP, source encoder MLP, candidate encoder MLP, then combined MLP head.

slides.note: This is the main technical architecture slide.

slides.headline: Training teaches the model to rank the true topology above alternatives
slides.subheadline: Candidate sets are built per phase, with the true topology always included as the positive label.
bullets: Perturbed true topologies add hard alternatives.
Random or guided MSTs broaden candidates.
Top-1 accuracy checks exact first choice.
slides.note: Emphasise exact topology accuracy and selected-edge F1 as complementary metrics.

slides.headline: Candidate ranking is strong; physics is the next lever
slides.subheadline: The candidate ranker substantially improves over direct edge prediction and sets up the physics-informed extension.

metrics.label: Next steps: compare, refine, scale, stress test

bullets: Stress test noise and partial observability.
slides.cta: Next: improve physics residuals and robustness testing
slides.note: Close with direction: voltage-drop consistency, current smoothness, and radial monotonicity.
